prompt_md: Your team ships an autonomous agent that can read customer records and also issue refunds via a `do_refund` tool. During review, a security architect flags that a malformed request once convinced the model to issue a large refund to the wrong account — the only safeguard was a prompt line saying "only refund valid orders." Given what you know about bounding agent autonomy, what is the best way to prevent high-impact, irreversible refunds from going wrong, and why?

decision_choices: Require human approval for refunds above a threshold, enforced in the application before the tool executes
Rewrite the prompt to state the refund rule more emphatically and add more examples
Increase the agent's max-iteration limit so it has more chances to reconsider
Lower the model's temperature so its decisions become more conservative
reason_choices: Because an architectural permission boundary cannot be overridden by the model, while a prompt instruction can be misread or bypassed
Because a more emphatic prompt is guaranteed to be obeyed on every request
Because more iterations let the model eventually catch its own mistakes
Because lower temperature removes the possibility of incorrect tool calls
